item.title: awww, they were roommates!
item.dc:creator: m4uboy

item.title: It's time for yet another unhinged XL Tuesday CeleryDump for the good & bad people of Imgur
item.description:
greetings online friends.. i hope you had a frank and productive upvote day
i absolutely do not know what time it is
there will be hell to pay
good times
nothin like a good sit n scream
just go to mcdonalds damn
as is tradition
i guess
okey dokey
impressive, very nice
no specified ending time = things might get crazy
i loved and still love these things
dang espn
i've done it (amsterdam)
what would you choose? octopus, or everything that is not an octopus?
i hope so
don't threaten me with a good time
that's all for this one.. have a great night gang!!
item.dc:creator: CeleryMan7

item.dc:creator: MermaidNinja

item.title: stay safe!
item.description: EDIT: Someone posted this awhile back about kicking out the tail lights: ?Kicking out the taillight is not likely going to get you through to the outside unless you're extremely strong and can bust out the entire light panel. What you're really trying to do is knock out the taillight's wiring so that they will get pulled over. There's a bigger chance of you getting them pulled over than punching through the entire taillight unit.? Thank you for all the useful tips! (originally posted by @MonStar1203 on 2023-07-31 19:24:43)
item.dc:creator: ThisTimeLastYear

item.title: Is A.I. Art Stealing from Artists?
item.description: According to the lawyer behind a new class-action suit, every image that a generative tool produces “is an infringing, derivative work.” https://www.newyorker.com/culture/infinite-scroll/is-ai-art-stealing-from-artists #the_more_you_know
item.dc:creator: Luvlyquants

item.title: Ireland women's rugby at the olympics

item.dc:creator: IRCBrink

item.title: Sometimes you have to play "hide and seek" with names and information on an old headstone...
item.description:
Old headstones are beautiful, but they are, well... old. They had a different culture for building and engraving headstones. This can lead to confusion for a visitor in current times, where they see a headstone without a name??? It's also often the case that time has taken its toll on a headstone, making them difficult to read. There are a few tips that can make it a *smidge* more visible, and I'm going to cover that as well! . . *Every time I say "old headstone", someone from the UK makes a comment like "You know OP is from America when they think a headstone from the 1800's is old." Yes, there are much older graves in Europe and other places around the world. However, a headstone that's over a century old is still old, and needs to be handled with care.
Quick recap: I do volunteer headstone restoration in my local cemetery. Link to the post with this meme: https://imgur.com/gallery/i-ve-got-to-break-free-vXmpn0U That being said, while most restorationists are focused on standing headstones, I'm almost entirely focused on ground level stones. I'll get into the particulars at the end of this post.
Abbreviated headstones are confusing to people who haven't encountered them (no judgement, I was confused when I first visited an old cemetery). Many graves mark a "burial area" with a large stone that features the family name, and the individual names of deceased members of the family. An abbreviated headstone is actually a "foot headstone", which acts as a marker for where a family member is specifically buried. Using the picture as an example, the Loomis Family is buried in this area of the cemetery. AJL's footstone marks the exact spot where Amandly James Loomis' coffin was buried. I know I just said a whole lot of words, so the next image is a little drawing that will hopefully provide a better visual.
Hopefully, that makes a little more sense! The family headstone is a list of who is buried at the plot, and the individual foot stones are where their specific coffins are located.
I wish I'd gotten another picture of the sides of this headstone. S.T stands for Samuel Taylor, but you'll notice that there's only one name on this side of the stone? Why put a big family monument for only one person? Psych! If you look on the other sides of the headstone, you'll find more names.
Sometimes you'll find a weird headstone that mentions everything but the last name. The headstone in front of it gives the last name. In this case, Raymond E's last name is Schortmann.
It's not a consistent thing, but small headstones like this sometimes will have more information about the person on the back. You might see a stone as beautiful as this, but with only an inscription and no name?? Check the back. The name might be engraved there.
I've encountered a few headstones where there's one family name on one side, and another family name on the other side. I don't know the particulars of why the grave is built like that, but my guess is that the Strachan Family is related to the Corry Family (maybe cousins?) Still, if you see footer headstones on their own, check the other side of the nearest large headstone. You might find the names there. . As a side note: polished pink granite (I *think* that's pink granite?) headstones will last forever, but I'm not sure if that makes much of a difference because you can't fucking read them. Grey granite is a little easier.
If you see everything except a first name, chances are the name is carved on the top of the headstone. Before someone says "Duh! It's right there!" Old headstones can fade, so it's easy to miss.
I've found some headstones that are literally just a last name with no information on the back, but not often. It's mostly the case that the names of the specific family members are engraved on the back of the stone.
I've seen many people understandably express confusion at a missing death date. I wish we had more people alive from the early 1900's. I'm sure they'd have so many stories to tell! However, in 2024, it's more likely the case that this person is deceased. . There are a few possibilities for a missing death date: - The person left the area, and are buried somewhere else - The family couldn't afford the extra engraving - Divorce. Ex: Florence divorced Everett, and had serious qualms with being buried next to him (no need to update a family plot that she's "noped" out of) - The deceased person was the last living family member, so there wasn't anyone to update the stone
Can't read a grave? First thing to try is to check either Find A Grave, or the cemetery's website. They might have information on it. If you can't find it, here's a couple of things you can do that might help you see them better. . NOTE: You might not be able to read the whole thing. Weathered graves are still weathered. But if you can make out a few letters of a name, a birth/death date, an age of death, etc... you might be able to look them up and find out more about them!
Harry's stone is legible, and in relatively good condition. The grave next to him was another matter. I think it was his wife's. Both sides of the headstone are blank, with the only name being carved into the top instead. Old headstones that aware awaiting restoration are a little worse for wear. If it's almost entirely illegible, you're probably not going to be able to read the whole thing, no matter what you do. It's not entirely hopeless, though. GENTLY pouring water on a weathered grave will darken the stone, and you might be able to make out a few words. In some occassions, you might even be able to read the whole thing! That being said, there's a catch to this. (See next image)
To be fair, I had an easier time reading the engraved name: "SARAH." However, it's mostly just a mess. While it's tempting to get a rubbing because it's "badly weathered," please don't! Sarah's headstone is DEFINITELY one that can be restored - getting a rubbing might chip off stone. Don't mess with the lichen, either. Lichen isn't loose grass. It's attached itself tight to the stone. Imagine that you've bitten down hard on a steak. Someone comes and tries to tug the steak out of your mouth. It will tear. With lichen, these little tears are microscopic, but it GREATLY damages the integrity of the stone, as improperly removing lichen is similar to tearing a steak out of your mouth. With Sarah's grave, try to read what you can without touching it, and leave the rest to a restorationist.
Telephoto lenses focus solely on the object in front of them, and blur out the rest of the background. This can make the details of a headstone slightly easier to read, as there isn't a bunch of detail behind it. You can also take a photo at an angle and use flash. Shining extra light on the engravings (whether using flash on a camera, or shining a bright flashlight) can help make a headstome more visible.
Polished granite can be a bitch to read. Grey polished granite is much easier, but still a bit of a pain. Sometimes using a telephoto lens, or “portrait mode” on your phone camera can really make these engravings much more legible.
There are headstones that you won't be able to read at all. Time is good at fading everything into obscurity. There are certain clues you can use to get a rough guess on who the headstone belongs to. Any headstone with a lamb on top of it belonged to a child between the 1800's and early 1900's. The most recent lamb headstone that I've encountered was from 1950. However, the most common 20th century lamb graves that I've encounted are from 1900 - 1928. See if you can read the name of the headstone next to it. Chances are, they're the baby's parents. You can look them up, and maybe find a name in a family obituary.
*dig it up was the wrong choice of words. I meant don't try to unbury it from the weeds and soil. I do not endorse graverobbing lol. . I'm gonna work on clearing up this sweet baby sometime soon. Charlotte’s stone can be read if you pluck away some of the overgrown weeds. That being said, ONLY focus on uncovering hidden information. Don’t try to clear up the whole stone - that’s going to require manual labor that’s better left to someone with both permission and training.
This one is not as simple as lifting the grass. A headstone like this would take a trained person a minimum of an hour and a half to get it completely uncovered and cared for (at least, that's how long it would take me if I was working on my own). Trying to unearth this one without proper tools and training could result in damaging the stone or injuring yourself. It’s better to leave this one alone. Speaking of avoiding injury, let's talk about graveyard safety!
Headstones are MUCH HEAVIER than they look. If one looks unstable, it's extremely important that you admire it from a distance. You're not going to walk off an injury caused by a fallen headstone. If it falls on your feet or arm, that's a broken bone. If it falls on your head, that's a concussion at the very least.
It is SO TEMPTING to get a closer look at headstone like Thomas', because you'd definitely be able to read the rest. Unfortunately, it's better to just leave it be. Thomas is tilting forward a lot more significantly than this photo shows. Behind him is an exposed base to what I assume is another headstone. Even though it's unlikely that this exposed base belongs to Thomas, it's way too close to him. Thomas might be okay, but he could also fall forward under the right circumstances. If you're in the way during those circumstances, that's going to hurt. . . As a side note: there are times where a standing headstone is welded to two bases instead of one. I don't think that's the case with Thomas, but I wanted to mention that anyway.
Jennie's headstone is EXTREMELY dangerous. It does seem that she has a little bit of extra support from the dirt propping it up. I don't recommend getting close to a headstone like her's, but it might be okay. WHATEVER YOU DO, DO NOT TRY TO CLEAR UP THE DIRT THAT'S COVERING UP THE REST OF THE HEADSTONE. THAT MIGHT BE THE ONLY THING KEEPING IT FROM TOPPLING OVER ON TOP OF YOU.
Frederick's headstone is fortunately quite legible. That being said, he's also propped up against a slope, and you can see the dirt eroding beneath it. Slope stones that are propped up against a fallen base are ones that you absolutely need to admire from a large distance. While this photo looks close up, I was actially standing at least five feet away from it. I did relent and get a close up image of the front of his stone, but even that was from some distance.
The only thing that I can read: Died (Feb?) 6, 1918 Ae 8 MOS. . This baby's headstone is fortunately falling in the opposite direction. I don't think they're going to hurt someone, but its condition makes it a good example of other potentially dangerous headstones like this. Standing headstones are welded to the base with screws. As time goes on, these screws deteriorate, and the headstone falls off. In this image, you can see the badly rusted screws bending backwards. Baby's headstone is very close to toppling over. Don't physically touch a headstone like this. One small push could snap the screws, and down it goes. ESPECIALLY don't touch it if the screws are bending forward. If they snap, they'll fall on your foot/hand, and that's a broken bone. . . There is one extremely helpful thing you can do for graves like Thomas, Jennie, Frederick, and Baby - report their condition to the groundskeeper.
Writing notes like this about badly damaged graves is something that anyone can do to help an old grave, and it's EXTREMELY helpful! Cemeteries are big. Mine has a recorded 4,000+ burial spots. Some headstones get unintentionally missed, and a thorough note from a good samaritan can make them aware of the problem!
This is the note that I wrote to the groundskeeper in the cemetery that I volunteer at. Hopefully this note gives a good visual on how to thoroughly report a damaged headstone! . Okay, the last thing in this post is something I'm including from personal experience of posting about my volunteering adventures.
I want to address the "clout" thing. I was doing this before I started posting about it. I was even taking pictures before I was posting about it, because I always kept a visual record of before/after I cared for a headstone. I starting sharing it online when I was frantic to prove to Imgur that I wasn't a serial killer lol. A couple of years ago, I got snubbed by my dad for some quality time. I was lonely and sad, so I made a post inviting people to hang out with me in the cemetery without thinking about the implication. There were quite a few commenters expressing concern about my intentions. I made a follow up post explaining that I volunteer in a cemetery. Link: https://imgur.com/gallery/i-just-wanted-to-add-little-more-info-to-yesterday-s-invitation-D6qvEQ1 I got requests to share more of my work, and I was happy to share something that brought me so much joy. I didn't start it because of the internet, and the internet wouldn't have even known about it if my dumbass hadn't unintentionally made myself look like a serial killer lol. . . Then there's the main reason that I began doing this project. Yes, there is some respect for the dead, but there's a reason that I'm mostly focused on ground-level headstones. Believe it or not, it's to protect the living.
Yes, there is a beauty in letting nature reclaim a headstone and let it fade into time. There’s also a sense of vindication when a shitty person disappears and is totally forgotten. However, some headstones are so sunken in that you can barely see them. I’ve tripped on a few headstones myself and nearly rolled on my ankle. The majority of people that I’ve encountered in the cemetery are elderly people visiting a recently departed friend/spouse. Some of these elderly people were shaking, relying on canes, and especially frail. If they trip and fall, they could get SEVERELY hurt. I certainly try to be respectful of the headstones I work with - but respect for the dead is secondary. If Bob was a terrible person, I’m still unearthing his stone. 80 year old Louise didn’t do anything to anyone. She's only at the cemetery to visit her husband. Why should she be mangled by his sunken headstone just to spite him? The answer: she shouldn't be. . There are other ways to spite Bob: - Walk by and glare at his grave. - Give him the finger. - Spit on the grass next to his grave - Write a scathing note and leave it next to his headstone Bob does not have to be remembered fondly in order to be visible. Being visible doesn't even need to be about him. Keeping him visible prevents him from hurting people beyond the grave. It's stopping his cycle of abuse. . . *I don't recommend doing anything that would count as vandalism to a headstone. Spiting dead people isn't worth jail time.
Old cemeteries are THE BEST! I hope these little tips on finding out more about the headstone will help you enjoy it even more! :)

item.title: Union pays off
item.description: UPS worker here, that potential strike that was all over the news? It got me a 50% pay increase so now I'm solidly middle class, and will be able to actually take vacations soon. Don't care about upvotes, just wanted to celebrate with a crappily made meme. (originally posted by @Nagant on 2023-07-31 23:10:02)

item.dc:creator: roaduardo

item.title: People tend to not want to believe acts of kindness, so I'm sharing some OG kindness that happened to my kid?
item.description: We were down by the local funfair, my son has sensory issues and speech issues...he really wanted to get on this ride by himself so obviously I agreed... At the start when it started to move he was a bit unsure. However the young lad controlling it spotted it and asked for a high 5 when he went passed!! My son was over the moon and kept it up each time they went past!! No I don't have the first high 5 because I was talking to my son instead of having my phone out, but as soon as I saw what was happening and how my son was back to being his happy self I recorded! Obviously I thanked the the lad working and told him he was amazing! But acts of kindness are all around us, they exist, people can be great! My son was soooo happy and got off the ride laughing...not to mention more confident!! Edit : question mark shouldn't be in the title, I must have hit ? Instead of ! ...my bad!
item.dc:creator: DumpingSh1tandBeingKind2

item.dc:creator: kaosnole95

item.title: Mistakes have been made
item.description: I decided to add some fresh jalapeños to my pasta tonight just for fun because i love all things spicy. I forgot to wash my hands before grabbing my purple friend from my bedside drawer much later in the night..... My va Jay Jay is burning something fierce right now. The hawk Tua was not kind to me this evening. Mistakes were made and I'm in so much pain. Pray for me friends.
item.dc:creator: JessicaRabbitWhoCollectsBadHabits
